one_line_takeaway: A training-free, LRP-guided weight-masking method that zeroes the incoming weights of a target class's most relevant neurons in one FC layer, matching retrain-from-scratch unlearning quality (At~0, Ag near-intact, MIA Fr up to 1.00) at a fraction of the time and memory, but demonstrated only on small single-class image-classification cases without formal guarantees.
key_finding: Neuronal Path Perturbation matches retrain-from-scratch on unlearning quality while being orders of magnitude cheaper. It drives target-class accuracy At to ~0 (0.00 MNIST, 0.03 CIFAR-10, 0.01 CIFAR-100, 0.00 mini-ImageNet), keeps remaining-class accuracy Ag essentially at the retrained level (0.97/0.97/0.82/0.83), and reaches MIA forgetting rate Fr = 1.00 (0.99 on CIFAR-10) equal to retraining. On mini-ImageNet it uses 0.93 GB vs retraining's 6.51 GB and finishes in ~1.35 s vs 2865.87 s. Ablations show a threshold effect: At collapses only once Mp passes a dataset-dependent point (~16-20% for low-class-count MNIST/CIFAR-10, ~10% for CIFAR-100/mini-ImageNet), which the authors attribute to greater representational redundancy in few-class models; the overlap study finds pairwise class-neuron overlap Xmost@2 < 0.5 and X@10 = 0 (no neuron serves all ten sampled classes).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a training-free, zero-shot class-unlearning method for image classifiers built from two steps the authors own: (1) Layer-wise Relevance Analysis, which runs Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) over 'unseen' samples of the target class (drawn from the test set or generated, never the original training data) to score how relevant each neuron is to that class, aggregating per-sample top-k neurons by occurrence frequency into a ranked list S_l of strongly relevant neurons; and (2) Neuronal Path Perturbation, which zeroes the incoming weights (from layer l-1) to those selected neurons in a single fully-connected layer, severing the class's classification path without retraining or fine-tuning. The stated selling points are explainability (LRP tells you which neurons are cut and why), privacy (no access to the original training set, i.e. zero-shot in the sense of Chundawat et al. [9]), and efficiency (no gradient updates, only weight masking). This is distinct from the cited related work: SISA [10], Boundary Unlearning [5], SCRUB [13], SSD [14], Amnesiac [24], and zero-shot noise/gated-transfer [9] are prior methods the authors compare against or contrast with, not their contribution; the paper positions its novelty as operating at the individual-neuron level within one FC layer rather than at the sample/class/feature-pruning level.
threat_model: The paper gives an explicit but mild actor/usage model (Sec. IV.A) rather than a strong adversarial one. Actors: a user who knows their data trained the model, can request unlearning of a whole class, and expects proof it was removed; and a model owner who canNOT directly access user data, sees only the class distribution, and must produce a class-unlearned model plus verification. The privacy goal is to avoid re-exposing the original training data during unlearning (hence 'zero-shot' via unseen/generated samples). The one genuinely adversarial element is the membership inference attack (MIA) used to VERIFY forgetting: an SVM is trained on the target model's posterior probabilities to recognize unlearned-class members, and the forgetting rate Fr is the fraction of unlearned points this attack fails to flag. So the adversary is a posterior-based membership-inference attacker, and success means the unlearned model resists it (high Fr).
limitations: (1) Scope is image classification with CNN/FC heads only; despite motivating with LLMs the method is never tested on transformers or language models, and it fundamentally targets a single FC layer. (2) 'Zero-shot' means no original TRAINING data, but the method still needs representative unseen samples OF the class to be forgotten (36 MNIST and 33 mini-ImageNet images here); it is one-class-at-a-time on tiny forget sets. (3) The unlearning 'guarantee' is weight masking plus a first-order Taylor heuristic (Claims 1-4), not a formal or certified guarantee, and the weights are zeroed rather than retrained, so residual information in other layers is not addressed. (4) Verification relies on a single SVM posterior-based MIA; no stronger membership inference, relearning/fine-tune-attack, or robustness analysis is run, so high Fr may overstate true forgetting. (5) Key hyperparameters Mp and N are dataset-dependent and appear hand-tuned with no principled selection rule. (6) Some baselines are weak (e.g. Neuronal path noise GN/LN collapse Ag to 0.00 on mini-ImageNet), and there is a text/table inconsistency on the mini-ImageNet runtime (text says 0.68 s, Table VI says 1.35 s).

datasets_used: MNIST (28x28, ALLCNN; unlearn class 1 using 36 unseen images)
mini-ImageNet (256x256, VGG-16 pretrained on ImageNet, 1000 classes; unlearn class 0 using 33 images)
quantitative_results: MNIST, proposed method: At=0.00, Ag=0.97, Fr=1.00, 0.64 GB, 0.68 s (retrain: 0.00/0.97/1.00/0.74 GB/71.37 s)
mini-ImageNet, proposed method: At=0.00, Ag=0.83, Fr=1.00, 0.93 GB, 1.35 s (retrain: 0.00/0.83/1.00/6.51 GB/2865.87 s)
MNIST ablation (epsilon rule, Top-50): At falls 0.99 -> 0.005 as Mp rises 0% -> 16% while Ag stays ~0.96-0.97
CIFAR-100 ablation (epsilon, Top-150): At 0.96 -> 0.01 -> 0.00 at Mp 0% / 10% / 12.5% with Ag held at 0.81

Neuron-overlap (Table XI): pairwise class overlap Xmost@2 < 0.5 on all four datasets; X@10 = 0 everywhere; at most 6 (MNIST), 7 (CIFAR-10), 5 (CIFAR-100), 9 (mini-ImageNet) classes share a single neuron
